The main reason is that `Mle` itself is not directly storing the RLOC16 value and `GetRloc16()` actually gets it from `Mac` using `Get<Mac::Mac>().GetShortAddress()`. OK, so I took a closer look at fast atomic add to understand why it needs the numel, and the answer is that the fast atomic add trick is solely for half precision additions, and works by turning a half-size atomic (which is slow) into a double size half2 atomic (which is fast), where the half (ha!) of half2 which is not updated is zeroed. However, if the atomic update is at the very bounds of the tensor in question, we're not allowed to do this, since it may not be valid to do a memory access out of bounds.

This means that we don't need the "true" numel of the tensor in question, we only need to approximately what is out of bounds or not. Similarly, you want the base pointer to be the very beginning of the tensor.

It can be useful if we want to pass an allocated object (pass the object and its ownership) to the user in an OT public API (which is C based and does not have notion of smart pointers or `RetainPtr` and works with raw pointer).  

I know some libraries choose to not provide this for the fear of being incorrectly used (which make sense for more general purpose libraries like STL that can be used by developers of all levels of experience).  For example,  `std::shared_ptr` does not have it, but [`boost::intrusive_ptr`](https://www.boost.org/doc/libs/1_60_0/libs/smart_ptr/intrusive_ptr.html) provides `detach()` and also the [`retain_ptr`](http://www.open-std.org/jtc1/sc22/wg21/docs/papers/2018/p0468r1.html) proposal for STL  provides this.  I think within OT we can make sure that it is not incorrectly used.

<details><summary>Reproducer</summary>

```python
import numpy as np
import timeit
from matplotlib import pyplot as plt
import pandas

N = 1_000_000
NRUNS = 5

index = pandas.RangeIndex(N)

def get_positions(index, percentage):
    n_elements = int(len(index) * percentage)
    indexer = list(range(n_elements))
    return list(np.random.choice(indexer, n_elements, replace=False))


fns = {
    "RangeIndex.__getitem__(python_list)": lambda index, indexer: index[indexer],
    "RangeIndex.__getitem__(np.array(python_list))": lambda index, indexer: index[
        np.array(indexer, dtype=np.int64)
    ],
}

X = np.arange(0, 101, 10)

results = {key: [] for key in fns.keys()}

for x in X:
    print(f"{x}%")
    python_list = get_positions(index=index, percentage=x / 100)
    print(f"\tDEBUG: list length is {len(python_list)}")
    for name, fn in fns.items():
        print(f"\tRunning '{name}' fn...")
        time = timeit.timeit(lambda: fn(index, python_list), number=NRUNS) / NRUNS
        results[name].append(time)

ax = plt.axes()

lines = [ax.plot(X, Y, label=label)[0] for label, Y in results.items()]
ax.legend(handles=lines)

ax.set_xlabel("Percentage of values to take")
ax.set_title(f"Index length is {N}")

plt.savefig(f"nelem_index{N}.png")

```

</details>

The issue I am pointing is with the generation of the TLVs. Every time a new device joins the network the parent needs to send the joiner entrust message with Network Key TLV. This means we will need the network key as a literal string to create this TLV. There are two approaches to this, either wee create the TLV and store it in the NVM or create this TLV on every boot / key change.

I don't think storing the TLV in NVM is a good option as this defeats the purpose of using key refs, as the network key is still stored in plain text as part of the TLV. So we would need to create this TLV on every boot, this is possible only if the network key is exportable, at least in the FTDs.

The most probable scenario is they use our updated setup script properly, and then run `PYTHON=python3.5 ./pants`.

This is totally valid in the setup repo to do and should always be valid. We don't want the setup script to pin which versions Pants supports, especially because it's so difficult to get people to consume updates to the script since they curl it and often modify after.

It is also totally valid to set in `pants.ini` the value `pants_engine_python_version: 3.5`. The checker doesn't belong there, because it's easy to circumvent unintentionally (see https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/7363#discussion_r264519327).

Pants _might_ work with 3.5, but it also might not and could be buggy. We should at least warn users they're tredding dangerous waters. If they choose to use the bypass and try it out, totally fine with us. Better UX than randomly hitting `SyntaxError`s and bugs from using an unsupported version, and them having no idea why.

Covariance rules are a little complicated to grok. Essentially it comes down to this sort of behaviour:

``` python
def cannot_accept_non_optional(items: List[Optional[int]]) -> None:
    items.append(None)

def can_accept_non_optional(items: Sequence[Optional[int]]) -> None:
    # Here items is immutable (from a type system perspective), so while
    # the code inside the function needs to cope with `None`s, they can't
    # leak out
    print(len(items))

items = [42]  # type: List[int]

cannot_accept_non_optional(items) # bad
can_accept_non_optional(items) # ok

for item in items:
    print(item * 2)
```

We can't have docker running like this because it leaves containers hanging around even when the parent process terminates. We introduce logic to avoid this kinds of problems in CI  https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/pull/12381

My suggestion is to use one of this two options:
- Use https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/ci/build.py as it's done in https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/ci/docker_cache.py to run the tests safely, build the images in parallel. This is probably the fastest route that is safe but introduces some coupling between CI and CD.
- Abstract the cleanup logic in build.py in a module which is then used from CI and CD with the specific commands.
- Duplicate the logic from build.py and docker_cache.py for this task (copy & paste) and adjust for the specific case. A downside is that introduces similar logic to be maintained but not coupling.

If on doubt, we can choose option 1 and go from there.


There's a brief discussion of why including `shell=True` is nice for users at https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/2676#issuecomment-198087749 but it doesn't get into the potential security concerns.

I don't think it's a problem though. The danger of the `shell` option is [shell injection](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Code_injection#Shell_injection) as described at https://docs.python.org/3.7/library/subprocess.html#security-considerations. I think this is a real problem if you're trying to use user input in a limited way, such as passing user defined arguments to another program.

Certbot, however, is literally letting users define arbitrary commands and we'll run it for them. They don't need to do any fancy shell injection. If they're able to control the string of the hook, they can put any malicious code there directly.

Instead of manually changing the version, you can use the `bumpversion` tool to keep version numbers synced everywhere they are referecenced in the repo. The Makefile has commands to help this process. For example when you run `make bumpversion-minor` it will find the current version in the `setup.cfg` which in this case should be `4.0.1`. Then it will determine that applying a patch means the new version will be incremented to `4.1.0`. It looks at all the files and patterns specified in the `setup.cfg` to replace told version with the new version. In our case, it will update the version in the `setup.py` and and` __init__.py`. It then replaces the current version with `4.1.0` and creates a new git commit with a tag as a reference for the exact commit of the release.

It might be easier to wait until after the PR has been merged and then performing the version bump directly on master branch before releasing, but I'm not 100% sure.

I think this might also mean adjusting how we use the changelog. What if we keep a section at the top that accumulates a list of changes from all the PRs, and when we decide to do a release we make a new section with the incremented version number and the date and move all the changes into the versioned section? Then the accumulating process would start again

Sorry, for not making myself clear enough. I didn't mean to suggest a range without an upper bound such as `plyvel>=1.0.5"`.

Because I think that one can in fact become problematic. Current plyvel works under the assumption of having a `leveldb` of version `1.2` or higher on the system. An unbounded `plyvel` dependency might even install a `plyvel` version `2.x` which may depend on a `leveldb` `2.x` (hypothetical future versions) and with breaking changes things might fall apart then.

I'd pick one of these:

- `plyvel>=1.1.0, <2` (if we trust plyvel does semver right)
- `plyvel>=1.0.5, <2` (if we trust plyvel does semver right)
- `plyvel>=1.1.0, <1.2.0` (more conservative)
- `plyvel>=1.0.5, <1.2.0` (more conservative)

From what I understand, Gmsh keeps track of which entities (geometric objects in the .geo file) points and cells have been assigned to:
* in the $Nodes section, the nodes are grouped into these entities. Since the entity numbering starts from 1 in each dimension, the dimension of the entity of the node must be stored. 
* the $Elements section is similar, but it contains only entities for which cells were generated (e.g. only those decleared Physical in gmsh lingo). Thus the entity information for cells is a subset of the full entity set.

The $Entities section is (I believe) generated from the entities of nodes, with physical tags and boundary representation from the cells. I have no idea how all this is handled internally in gmsh, but when I tried to get away with preserving less information, gmsh could not process the file.

We considered doing concrete Django model inheritance, but instead used abstract inheritance because the complexity required to do data and schema migrations was too great to justify the costs and risks.  Abstract inheritance still gives us the benefits of:
1. flexibility to have subsidy-specific methods and fields on the child models.
2. common behavior of methods via the base class.
3. common and unique `uuid` field for easy reference across systems (e.g. the uuid is what's stored on ledger transactions to commit them).

Mitigation for reporting/analytics: can easily write a view to aggregate these two tables into a single relation with e.g. a type column to easily distinguish between subsidy types.

DB read-only mode:

Both modes exercise the SQL-based read path within the plugins, which is what we actually want to test. Does ROOT change the reference count when you return a PyObject? (And thus, we'd have to be careful not to overcount...)

```python
>>> import ROOT
>>> ROOT.gInterpreter.Declare("""
... #include <Python.h>
... 
... PyObject* g(PyObject* obj) {
...     std::cout << "HERE " << Py_REFCNT(obj) << std::endl;
...     return obj;
... }
... """)
True
>>> import sys
>>> some_object = (1, 2.2, "three")
>>> sys.getrefcount(some_object)
2
>>> another_reference = ROOT.g(some_object)
HERE 3
>>> sys.getrefcount(some_object)
2
>>> del some_object
>>> anoth *** Break *** segmentation violation
```

Nope!

So the safe way to do this is to manually increment:

```python
>>> import ROOT
>>> ROOT.gInterpreter.Declare("""
... #include <Python.h>
... 
... PyObject* g(PyObject* obj) {
...     std::cout << "HERE " << Py_REFCNT(obj) << std::endl;
...     Py_INCREF(obj);
...     return obj;
... }
... """)
True
>>> import sys
>>> some_object = (1, 2.2, "three")
>>> sys.getrefcount(some_object)
2
>>> another_reference = ROOT.g(some_object)
HERE 3
>>> sys.getrefcount(some_object)
3
>>> del some_object
>>> another_reference
(1, 2.2, 'three')
```

The only way to hit this `except` block (modulo bugs) is if the `run_sync_in_worker_thread` gets cancelled. Cancellation is always a `BaseException`, not an `Exception`, so right now I think this is a no-op? Also, 

It's really not clear whether we even care about cancellation here... since this is a system task, the only way it can get cancelled is if the entire `run` is shutting down, either because the main task has already finished, or because there was an internal error and we're crashing. So... I guess the only time it matters whether we call `result.event.set()` here is when someone is doing a *cancel-shielded* wait for a subprocess to finish? And if they do, we won't handle it correctly *anyway* – the user would expect a cancel-shielded wait to actually wait for the subprocess to exit, but we'll still abort the `run_sync_in_worker_task`, so we won't know when the subprocess exits. Maybe we need to resurrect #303 to handle this corner case? Or toggle the system task's shielding on and off depending on whether anyone is currently blocked in `Popen.wait`? Or use `threading.Thread` directly instead of trying to go through `run_sync_in_worker_task`? (...can we even reliably re-enter the trio thread once the system nursery is cancelled? The re-entry queue processor is also a system task...)

Man, I hate `waitpid`.

This is such an obscure use case that I don't think we need to worry about it right now. Trying to do a cancel-shielded wait for a subprocess isn't *too* ridiculous – `nursery.__aexit__` is one of the standard use cases for cancel-shielding, and people will want to do nursery-like things for managing subprocesses. So we might end up caring eventually. But right now we should just shrug and accept that it won't do exactly the right thing here. And if we don't care about that, we can make this whole function a lot simpler, like...

```python
try:
    result.outcome = await run_sync_in_worker_thread(...)
finally:
    # Paranoia to make sure we unblock any waiters even if something goes horribly wrong
    result.event.set()
```

The problem with checking iterables is that strings are iterables, and we definitely don't want to iterate strings. This is a more general issue that we should probably address by creating a package-wide `is_iterable` helper that checks for any iterable but a string. There are other places throughout the codebase where we'd need to make the same update.

That said, this has never been a problem in practice, so I don't consider it a high priority. `get()` is not really a place where users should be passing around iterables other than lists or tuples anyway, since you wouldn't know you could do that unless you read the documentation.

Async lookups don't make sense for Bedrock, since there's no actual lookup being performed, it's handled purely with `Address.parse_address`, which simply takes the string IP:Port and obtains the individual parts (IP and port).

Async behavior only makes sense with Java lookups, which require an additional SRV DNS record check, that does need an asynchronous version. This means that the base class should only include the synchronous lookup, since only that is shared across both servers, and therefore only sync version should be in the base class

I checked. It seems like a race condition. When we use `0.1` as the timeout in `ray.wait` then, `V2.handler` is executed before `V1.handler` and hence `requests.get` receives `2|PID` as the value. Basically the version update on Windows happens before `request.get` completes in case of `ref2`. Increasing `timeout` to `2.1` gives more time for completing execution of `requests.get` and hence this race condition is avoided. cc: @pcmoritz @simon-mo Let me know if anything is not clear.

P.S. I am not sure if we can make this stuff more safer because this condition can arise on any OS depending on the speed of `requests.get`. May be we can make the tests more safer like verifying the version in `def call(version, block=False)` before going for `requests.get`. execution.

These two classes serve very different purposes though. `ray.ml.Checkpoint` is a user-facing API to ship checkpoint data around and convert between different formats.

`TrackedCheckpoint` is a developer-facing API to track checkpoint metrics and storage location.

Storage location in `ray.ml.Checkpoint` is an internal construct and shouldn't usually be accessed. The only benefit we would get from wrapping around `ray.ml.Checkpoint` is easy conversion from dict to data - but this is a small and well-defined part. The main disadvantage is making it very hard to serialize `TrackedCheckpoints`, as per default path checkpoints are always serialized when shipped. We would have to override this behavior as well. Basically we would override most of the functionality in `ray.ml.Checkpoint` and only keep the dir-dict conversion and not use the other conversions. We would also have to change many internal APIs currently working with TrackedCheckpoint. This is very little benefit for a massive refactoring.

Yes, all arguments should have `options_list`. Even if no `option_list` specified, core will create `option_list` by `dest` since `dest` is mandatory for each argument. [Code in knack](https://github.com/microsoft/knack/blob/dc7b7d857ce3c11b2a3155b81f2470b3b579e7c9/knack/arguments.py#L45-L68):
```Python
class CLICommandArgument(object):


    NAMED_ARGUMENTS = ['options_list', 'validator', 'completer', 'arg_group', 'deprecate_info', 'preview_info',
                       'experimental_info', 'default_value_source']


    def __init__(self, dest=None, argtype=None, **kwargs):
        """An argument that has a specific destination parameter.

        :param dest: The parameter that this argument is for
        :type dest: str
        :param argtype: The argument type for this command argument
        :type argtype: knack.arguments.CLIArgumentType
        :param kwargs: see knack.arguments.CLIArgumentType
        """
        self.type = CLIArgumentType(overrides=argtype, **kwargs)
        if dest:
            self.type.update(dest=dest)


        # We'll do an early fault detection to find any instances where we have inconsistent
        # set of parameters for argparse
        if not self.options.get('dest', False):
            raise ValueError('Missing dest')
        if not self.options_list:  # pylint: disable=access-member-before-definition
            self.options_list = ('--{}'.format(self.options['dest'].replace('_', '-')),)
```

You’re right—it isn’t needed here. You can read `'_` as “infer the
correct lifetime”. The general feature is called [lifetime elision], and
the `'_` syntax was introduced in rust-lang/rfcs#2115; see conversation
for discussion, or the diff for the final text:

> - You can write `'_` to explicitly elide a lifetime, and it is deprecated to
>   entirely leave off lifetime arguments for non-`&` types

[lifetime elision]: https://doc.rust-lang.org/stable/reference/lifetime-elision.html

The reason that it’s here is that I write trait implementations by
typing `impl Display for MaskedCrc {}` and then asking rust-analyzer to
fill out method stubs for all required methods (`<Space>j1` in my
editor), which generates:

```rust
impl Display for Foo {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut std::fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> std::fmt::Result {
        todo!()
    }
}
```

Given that it’s optional, I don’t have a strong preference. In the
particular case of `fmt::Formatter`, I do actually find it helpful to
recall that `Formatter` is parameterized by a lifetime, since this
affects how you can use it and isn’t entirely obvious. As the RFC says:
“The `'_` marker makes clear to the reader that *borrowing is
happening*, which might not otherwise be clear.”

I’m also weakly inclined to deem it acceptable to write the code that
rust-analyzer generates, so that I don’t have to always correct it. But
if we want to have a policy of eliding lifetimes implicitly rather than
implicitly absent a strong reason, I would be open to considering that.

> Is current approach more convenient and general for many potential accelerations?
While I do think current approach is more general, it limits how data provider could optimize their own pipeline, since there is no order guarantee for them. On the other hand, LightGBM, since have the full control, can be optimized, like pipelining as you mentioned.

Idea here is, 
- If we trust user could build the best data provider against their own data storage traits, we should put limitations on the interface, so that data provider could get an garenteed data access pattern. This limits what lightGBM could do, yet maximizes global potential.
- If we don't, maybe we should keep using current general implementation, that we could do more (pipelining, prefetching, multiprocessing, distributed I/O, auto-tunnning, etc etc) and benefits everyone that implements it.
- I would say those two both has their benefits, if the user base is large enough, I would recommend implementing both. - (or even consider separate data sampling stage and parsing stage, allow the other one to be implemented in a total different way)

> For prefetching when reading full dataset, I think we can pipeline reading from the seq and push_rows here.

That seems like a very good idea. Are you implying multithreading? 

Large data reading could bounded by CPU (as long as they are not implemented in hardware offloaded fashion e.g. MPI), I'm not so sure if that would be the best.

How do you expect the `LivyBatch` object to be used?

The usage of `LivySession` as a context manager enables it to be automatically closed when the code block exits, but I'm not sure this makes sense for a non-interactive batch session.

Would it make more sense to drop the context manager behaviour and have something that works like:

```python
batch = LivyBatch(...)
batch.start()  # Actually creates the batch in Livy
batch.wait()  # Waits for a state indicating the batch is finished (either successfully or through an error)
batch.logs  # A `@property` that fetches the latest log lines
```

Keeping `batch.kill()` seems like a valuable method available to the user if their batch gets stuck.

I'd suggest that  `start` does not wait for the batch to actually begin - this was necessary for the interactive session in `LivySession` but does not seem so valuable here.

To sum up the discussion, we need something that can be serialized, which excludes `std::time::Duration` (intentionally abstract so users don't rely on an implementation) and `time::Timespec` (deprecated and not serde aware).
The issues with `float` are:
* potential loss of precision (academic in the case of span since the zipkin API only cares to the microsecond)
* potential annoyances for testability as they aren't supposed to be used for equality comparison

As an alternative, I could raise a PR to create a very simple crate that replaces `time::Timespec` and fills all our usecases (serialization, ffi friendliness, equality comparison, conversion from protobuf::Timestamp). This would avoid juggling between 4 time representations or so.

On the python side, we could also deal with u64, u32 for secs and nanosecs until the py_zipkin interface, and then convert to float.

> kvm_open* isn't likely to fail with EACCESS, as it doesn't open /dev/[k]mem as in the old days.
It will only fail if the library cannot allocate internal memory, or cannot open the kernel boot file. errno will be set appropriate.
The question is which exception should be raised - RuntimeError or OSError?

If `errno` is set then it's easy: just use `PyErr_SetFromErrno(PyExc_OSError)`. My understanding though is that `kvm_` functions do not set `errno`, so we need to parse `kvm_geterr`. As a general rule, if `errno` is not set, parse `kvm_geterr` result and:

- set `ESRCH` if the string indicates the process is gone (use `NoSuchProcess("...");`)
- set `EPERM` if the string indicates some lack of privileges (use `AccessDenied("...");`)
- raise/call `PyErr_NoMemory()` if the string indicates it could not allocate memory
- raise `RuntimeError` in all other circumstances; if you can, also include the result of `kvm_geterr`

This logic (3 `if`s, 1 `else`) should be handled by `convert_kvm_err` utility function, and (if you feel like it, while you're at it =)) reused by all functions that use `kvm_*` APIs.
Maybe `convert_kvm_err` could have another `if` condition, which is executed first:

```c
if (errno != 0)
       PyErr_SetFromErrno(PyExc_OSError)
````

...but this assumes that one of the previous `kvm_` calls **did** set `errno`, but again, my understanding is that they usually don't (the various man pages never mention `errno`).

> kvm_getprocs isn't likely to fail itself. If the search for the PID fails, cnt is set to 0 [...]

Then raise `NoSuchProcess("...");`

> [...] If the function itself returns NULL, it means that it was not able to allocate memory for the result, or the call parameters were invalid due to a programming error (&cnt pointing to invalid storage, kd invalid or NULL, meaning a previous kvm_open* did not suceed). We can raise RuntimeError or OSError if the call itself failed.

Mmmm... if we have a way to differentiate with `kvm_geterr` (is it ENOMEM? is it EINVAL? ....) then I would say to just stick with `convert_kvm_err` utility fun. 

> If the call reports cnt == 0, this maps to ESRCH ("no such process"). This will also happen if the process with the given PID exists, but sysctl security.bsd.see_other_uids == 0.

Damn... This I don't know. =)

> There is just one race which comes to my mind: When calling kvm_getprocs(), it may return success.
But before our call to kvm_getenvv(), we might have been preempted by the kernel, and the process in question might have terminated. In this case, our later call to kvm_getenvv() will return EINVAL, and we probably should remap this to ESRCH?

Mmm... hard call. I would say yes, let's map it to `ESRCH`.

I see. We don't **really** need to add varargs to argtypes at all. We only need to add the non-variadic arguments: https://docs.python.org/3/library/ctypes.html#calling-variadic-functions

However, we can't just remove the varargs, because ctypes converts `str` to `wchar_t*` by default: https://docs.python.org/3/library/ctypes.html#calling-functions

> None, integers, bytes objects and (unicode) strings are the only native Python objects that can directly be used as parameters in these function calls. None is passed as a C NULL pointer, bytes objects and strings are passed as pointer to the memory block that contains their data (char* or wchar_t*). Python integers are passed as the platforms default C int type, their value is masked to fit into the C type.

In my opinion, if we are going to add the varargs to argtypes, we should add all of the varargs to argtypes.

Correct me if I'm wrong, Pat, this is partly to check my own understanding...

When evaluating the fanout of a DBR operation, we must walk the condemned resource's dependencies in reverse, before the engine has seen them. There's nothing new here, we did this previously for DBR. Pat's twist is that we're now invoking Diff on these resources as we walk them.

Suppose we had a resource graph that looks like this:

```
    A
    |
    B
    |
    C
```

Where `B` is a provider resource. If `A` gets DBR'd, the engine is going to call `Diff` on `B` to understand if a change to `A` is going to have any effect. However, since we are operating in reverse, the engine has not seen `B` yet and, as such, has not had the opportunity to call `Check` on it. `Check` on a provider is what actually loads the provider. So, `r.GetProvider` will only succeed on providers that the engine has seen and called `Check` on.

In this case, `!ok` means "the engine has not yet called `Check` on this provider". Its presence in the checkpoint inductively implies that this reference isn't totally bogus, so it's safe to assume that this provider does exist, just later in the plan.

The remedy here is to just use the old provider for the given provider resource. The provider registry loads all providers on startup, so that provider is guaranteed to be there.

So the auto-refresh flow today is:
- hit a view with a token -->  if `MIN_REFRESH_INTERVAL` allows it, update expiry and hit the DB with a write.
- hit a view with a token -->  if `MIN_REFRESH_INTERVAL` denies it, don't update expiry and don't write to the DB.

What we'd ideally want for the refresh view (whether or not auto-refresh is active) is:
- hit the refresh view with a token --> refresh the token in every case by doing a single write to the DB

If auto-refresh is off, there is no issue.

If auto-refresh is on, hitting the refresh view will do this:
- if `MIN_REFRESH_INTERVAL` is expired, the auto-refresh will update the expiry and write to the DB at authentication time, next the view will do it again, resulting in 2 writes for nothing.
- if `MIN_REFRESH_INTERVAL` is not expired, only the view will write to the DB, 1 write everybody is happy.

The problem we need to solve is to avoid to have 2 consecutive useless writes to the DB...
Sadly I don't think we have access to the view or anything useful to identify the refresh view in the authenticate method (where `AUTO_REFRESH` happens)...

I would say, let the 2 DB write be and add a note in the doc that if you use `AUTO_REFRESH` and the refresh view together, a double write could happen.

This formula here is not correct.

unlocked, locked, and onchain unlocked are **disjoint** sets. So, it doesn't make sense to subtract the unlocked amount from the locked amount.

This is the meaning of these attributes:

- locked locks: Locks which have been introduced with a locked transfer, however the secret is not know yet.
- unlocked locks: Locks which the secret is known, but the partner has not unlocked it off chain yet.
- onchain unlock: Locks which the secret is known, the partner has not unlocked off chain, and the secret has been registered onchain before the lock has expired.

So, for the `unlock` call, the value that you can be received by a node is `onchain unlock`, the `locked` and offchain `unlock` goes back to the sender.

Another way to get these values, with a simpler formula, is to use the `locked_amount` from the balance proofs, which is the sum of all these locked amounts, and from it remove the `onchain unlock`.

By default, [the objects are cleaned up](https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/orm/session_api.html#sqlalchemy.orm.session.Session.commit): By default, the `Session` also expires all database loaded state on all ORM-managed attributes after transaction commit. This so that subsequent operations load the most recent data from the database. This behavior can be disabled using the `expire_on_commit=False` option to sessionmaker or the `Session`constructor.

The reason is that it's a Python package. User requirements — those required for the tool to work — are defined in `setup.py`. They're the packages that are installed along with `pip install thefuck`. That list also serves as a guide to dependency definition in other package managers. That until you realize that if init_blocks=max_blocks=2, you'll never hit 3 failed blocks.  So for executors like that, some special handling is needed. It turns out that all executors that submit "blocks" have init_blocks and max_blocks, so, for the current set of executors what you say is true. But init_blocks and max_blocks is not standardized in any way, so it would be unwise to pretend that all executors inheriting from StatusHandlingExecutors will magically have this working when the error handing in StatusHandlingExecutors works only if hasattr("init_blocks").

@twollnik
I can hear you and thanks for useful link.
I really appreciate it that you give me the good oppotunity to rethink about how to write with Python. As I really don't intend to offend you, I'd like to have my opinion(understanding) about `@classmethod` and `@staticmethod` heard.

---

For readability two decarator shouldn't be used in the same file but I don't think so.
**Mixing `@classmethod` and `@staticmethod` in the same file is not bad**, or rather, unifying `@classmethod` causes misunderstaindings, I think.

We shouldn't basically consider two decorators as the same things and it is better to use them depending on the situation, though redusing readability.

I think we should use respectively:
* `@classmethod`
  * Methods that access NOT to instance methods, instance variables(attributes) BUT to class variables and class methods
  * Methods that is used as **factory method** which generates new instance(like `Tweet.from_soup`)

* `@staticmethod`
  * Utility methods that is related to class
  * Generally `@staticmethod` is basically the same as utility global functions, however, differ in terms of **if they are bound to class** or not. By using `@staticmethod`, We can EXPLICITLY say "This utility function is for a certain class".

As above, I use them. The problem that "mixing @classmethod and @staticmethod is confusing" can be solved in the source code, for instance, by sorting methods.

Feel free to tell me if you think something about my opinion:)

so in other words, when a literal like `Foo({a: 1, b:2})` gets parsed, it has the members in a different order but still type checks as the same type as `Foo({b: 2, a: 1})`. the solution is to reorder the members either at parse-time or assignment-time, and I did the latter in this PR because we don't have good type refinement at parse-time.

`h3_set_to_multi_polygon` is just a reasonable binding representation of `h3SetToLinkedGeo`, which is part of the core C API. The pieces that go beyond the C API are:

**GeoJSON to H3**
- Accepting `MultiPolygon` input coordinates
- Accepting GeoJSON input rather than just coords
- Accepting `FeatureCollection` input

**H3 to GeoJSON**
- Outputting GeoJSON rather than just coords
- Outputting alternative transformations, e.g. a `FeatureCollection` of hexagons

Basically anything that knows about *actual* GeoJSON formats, rather than just coordinates, seems out of scope IMO - that's why we put this in a separate lib for JS.

This covers all the key-values that are provided as `field_adaptors`, I believe, essentially because we're getting this info from a `TargetAdaptor`, which will explicitly only use `HydrateableField` `@union` members as the `_kwargs` provided by `Struct` -- see `hydrate_targets`: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/1988587507cb2cb21efffac01ac9a8c3ebf291a5/src/python/pants/engine/legacy/graph.py#L497-L509

> In particular, if we change a default value in pants, or set a default with a flag or something, and the target doesn't set it, will the fingerprint change? Presumably it *should*, right?

Short answer: no, this fingerprint will not necessarily change, and yes, I absolutely think it should before we merge this.

Longer answer: Not all the things that contribute to `Target#fingerprint` do _not_ transfer to a `TargetAdaptor` (which subclasses `StructWithDeps`, which subclasses `Struct`), *only* the things which are marked as hydrateable fields. As you imply, this is possibly not what we want at all for this purpose, and my use of `TargetAdaptor` here might not be correct.

@stuhood do you have any insight on how to bridge this? Does it break the v2 build graph traversal model if we're able to get an instance of a real `Target` in order to get a more normal fingerprint (i.e. a fingerprint containing everything the target payload does)? Are the `Payload`/`Target` concepts necessarily v1-only/requiring a v1 build graph, or is it possible to avoid having to reimplement payloads for all targets? This is one reason why moving more of the Job data into separate ORMJob columns might be advantageous, as it would reduce the overhead for this kind of type checking.

If we had a top level `exception` column on the ORMJob table (that is nullable) then we don't need a general purpose parser to handle serialized `Exception` objects.

I think the main remaining issue then is that we use a `datetime` object as a function argument and we are expecting to be able to serialize/deserialize that. I think we could use a try/except to handle deserialization, using the Django utils parse datetime.

The Python JSONDecoder object allows passing `object_hooks` which try to do further deserialization, so, for example, we would try to coerce any strings to datetimes using the Django parse function, and just return the original string if it failed.

See the documentation here: https://docs.python.org/3/library/json.html#json.JSONDecoder and an example here: https://stackoverflow.com/a/10734224 (note they are using a different parsing function).

There's a lot going on here and I think some of it is confusing. Setting a field like `sys.stdout` doesn't inherently affect buffering (buffering is a property of the stream itself). Furthermore, the default stdio buffering behavior is affected by whether Python is started via `python -u`, so that's one way to disable buffering for spawned processes (such as workers). If you're trying to do this for the driver, though... I feel like it's the caller's responsibility to manage stdio, and to ensure they're redirected before the process is spawned. It seems a bit unexpected to pull the rug out from underneath the caller, since the caller (which may be the user) might deliberately set up stdio in some particular way. It might be good to discuss the approach to see if there might be a better way?

There is a _lot_ of path gymnastics in this PR. They all center around this final layout. The `pip install --prefix` scheme is the same as a venv layout:
```
venv/
  bin/
  include/
  lib/
    pythonX.Y/
      site-packages/
  <data>
```

The main trick is to use that scheme, since its non-buggy unlike `pip install --target`, but preserve the zipapp mode semantics by moving all the `site-packages` to the root and shifting everything remaining under `.prefix` since dotted names are never Python importable. At venv creation time this re-shuffle is reversed and a valid RECORD written out.

Fun fact, `pip` only started to (correctly) enforce those requirements [last](https://blog.python.org/2020/11/pip-20-3-release-new-resolver.html) [November](https://pyfound.blogspot.com/2019/12/moss-czi-support-pip.html).  It might be too soon to assume that everyone can depend on `pip` doing the right thing.

Regardless, any attempt for a dependent project to enforce a stricter version requirement would simply cause it from being dropped from Linux distributions that, for one reason or the other, do not yet ship the new cython-hidapi.  When this is necessary because of serious compatibility issues, it's obviously beneficial; but for a name change, not so much.

The only reasonable ways for dependent packages to deal with the warning, for next couple of years, are to handle it or suppress it entirely.  And handling it requires conditional behavior, either through the proposed `version` attribute (we cannot even assume `pip` is necessarily installed on users computers), or by `try: dev = hid.Device(); except AttributeError: dev = hid.device()`.

Yes, for datetime fields doing the query with `Q(scheduled_time=None)` gives exactly the same compiled SQL as `Q(scheduled_time__isnull=True)` does. But this is not true for all field types, for instance `JSONField`, where `fieldname=None` translates to Javascript Object Notation's `null`, where as `fieldname__isnull=True` translates to SQL's `IS NULL`.

Moreover, while `Q(scheduled_time=None)` may give the desired result, if one has to reverse the logic, `Q(scheduled_time!=None)` doesn't work for obvious reasons. So we have two way of spellings, `Q(scheduled_time=None)` to compile into `IS NULL` and `Q(scheduled_time__isnull=False)`  to compile into `IS NOT NULL`.

For Keil MDK, you'll need to create a [CMSIS Device Family Pack](https://open-cmsis-pack.github.io/Open-CMSIS-Pack-Spec). The CMSIS-Pack specification allows for a [datapatch element](https://open-cmsis-pack.github.io/Open-CMSIS-Pack-Spec/main/html/pdsc_family_pg.html#element_dbg_datapatch) to be defined to override the value returned by the hardware from an AP or memory read.

In fact, the example included in the documentation is exactly what you need:

```xml
<debug>
    <!-- Patch the ROM Table base address -->
    <datapatch  type=”AP”__dp="0" __ap="0" address="0xF8" value="0xE00FF003" info="AP BASE Register, ROM Table at 0xE00FF000"/>
</debug>
```

While pyocd supports CMSIS Packs, it doesn't have a data patch mechanism. Adding patch support would be a good way to work around the issue, it's more work than I have time for right now.

A good mid-term work around will be to manually fix-up the AP's ROM table base address value after it's read. This is done by wrapping the init call sequence tasks similarly to what you've done, but more focused. I'll reply in a little bit with the code to insert.

Since `__metaclass__` isn't used at all in Python 3, that's a non-starter.

TBH, I'm not entirely sure why it does that. (At a guess, it may be trying to collapse the list of implemented interfaces including from super classes at class creation time?)

1. a thread is pinned
2. enters the context block (`_locals.old = True`)
3. is manually unpinned (with `unpin_this_thread()`)
4. exits the context block (`not _locals.old == False` so no action is taken)
5. the thread will be unpinned when it reaches here

This new code will always restore the previous pinning state when exiting a context block. In my view this is more predictable, but I'm open to your opinion on this.

Note that the timeout interval we use after a tx is actually the current retry wait interval (wait time till the next retry).

If we are detached after this, we correctly resume with the same longer/new wait interval.

"How do we make `self.foo: str = "foo"` work but not `bar.foo: str = "foo"` for some random bar?"

although it turns out `mypy` does catch situations where these in-line type-hints happen in both cases where something gets type-hinted outside of the class.

The issue is that pep8 defines private functions as those which should only be used within the same class and derived. But in pyiron we have three levels: 

- user level - what is used in the notebook
- developer level - what is used in derived classes but should never be called from the user directly
- class level - what is only used within one class but not in other classes - _private functions_

Note that when an `Index` (such as `outindex`, `slicestarts`, and `slicestops`) are passed into a kernel, you have to pass the raw pointer (`.ptr().get()`) and its offset (`.offset()`). Both pieces of information are needed to get at logical index `i` of an array, since one `Index` may be sharing a buffer with another `Index`, starting at a different offset: logical index `i` is at `offset + i`. It's a downside of forcing everything through a C interface. If I had been more organized at the beginning, I might have defined some C structs to carry both pieces of information over, though it would still be non-standard syntax to call a function that adds the offset in every get/put.

Except for one special case, _output_ `Indexes` can be assumed to have offset = 0 (because you just allocated it before entering the kernel; of course the offset is 0!). The special case involves concatenation.

> The conceptual gap here is the `x_sources` Target would be a Target Generator when operating on source files, but a plain old `Target` that resolves to several (codegenned) source files when using an `output_sources` value. This makes it difficult to reason about the behaviour of this target type, and similarly difficult to document: This would significantly complicate the documentation for each of these `x_sources` targets. It would need to _explain_ that the target behaves significantly differently depending on whether you're using it for real files or to wrap generated files from elsewhere.

`TargetGenerator` is an API that we provide to target implementors, rather than necessarily limiting what we can do with targets. New logic can be added in [the spot where we implement `TargetGenerators`](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/dfbfd75f3b1ce1d4c53a639eaeda2254d75078d8/src/python/pants/engine/internals/graph.py#L212-L214) to trigger (*a form of: see below) codegen.

I'm not sure that there would be a mode switch in this case (and so no extra documentation for this case): if multiple files were provided, the target could act exactly as it would otherwise, by generating multiple `python_source` targets.

* The primary complication I see is that this should likely not use the codegen API, per-se. Instead, it seems like a separate `@union` for "things which can be used as source file inputs" could be added and used both for this use case, and for `http_source`. We don't necessarily want all codegen to be able to during the calculation of dependencies, and that would keep the graph simpler.

> Do you think that we need a more precise tool? I can imagine doing something like adding a useLegacyDiff property to ResourceOptions. That feels a little bit hacky, but would at least let users control this on a per-resource granularity.

My gut says no, but I defer to you here.  My thought about the environment variable was based by the following thoughts (which may or may not be correct):

1. Because we require changes across pulumi, the tf providers and the kubernetes provider, it's possible that we'll have a period of time where not everything is upgraded and things behave "strangely" (or everything does get upgraded but we discover some bugs which take us some time to address).  In this case being able to say: "I want to go back to the way things were before these changes" in a very blunt fashion seems useful.
2. We believe the new behavior is the correct long term decision for the product and that's the behavior we want long term.

If (2) is correct (and I think it is) then providing a first class way to opt out of this stuff on a per resource basis isn't something we would want to support long term.  So I'd rather not force folks add properties to ResourceOptions that we know we'd be deprecating and removing shortly.  Having this controlled outside the program and in a blunt fashion feels better to me because it makes it clearer that we believe the default behavior is correct and if you are opting out of it its because there's a bug you have to work around while we fix it.

I do see the appeal of having something more targeted here, but I worry about pushing something into the programming model in a more first class way that allows you to do something that long term we believe is the wrong behavior.

My suggestion is different:
- When building `RadioSpinel` for use with ZigBee (or any other protocol), we can still use the `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_MLE_MAX_CHILDREN` config to specify the size of the src match table used by `RadioSpinel` and we can set it to whatever make sense for that stack. 
   - The `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_MLE_MAX_CHILDREN` config is OpenThread (and Thread protocol) specific.
   - Other stacks probably have their own configs to define max children supported by the stack (they should not use `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_MLE_MAX_CHILDREN` as a way to define how many children they support).

---

A second suggestion (which I mentioned earlier) is to define a new spinel specific config for src match table size of `RadioSpinel`. This way we avoid any confusion with its association with `MAX_CHILDREN`:
- We can define a new spinel specific config `OPENTHREAD_SPINEL_CONFIG_MAX_SRC_MATCH_ENTRIES` in `openthread-spinel-config.h` (defines size of the  local src match table used by `RadioSpinel` when `RCP_RESTORATION` feature is enabled and used).
- We set the default value for this config to be `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_MLE_MAX_CHILDREN` (so it works correctly under OT and Thread stack).
- Other protocols can define whatevery value they want for size of the table using this config.


---

> The other option is to allow RCP to accomodate the bigger src match table when needed, and not really be concerned if the spinel copy of src match table is not up-to-date.

This is about host `RadioSpinel` and whether we want RCP restoration to function correctly or not. 
- If you want to use RCP restoration feature of `RadioSpinel` when used with other protocols/stacks, then we need to make sure it can keep a local copy of src match table that is the same as what is set on RCP. 
- That's why I asked earlier whether you intend to use the "RCP restoration feature" with other stacks (under multi-PAN).

What's the difference between this code and the equation in the reference?  I can't see it:

![image](https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/assets/57452607/4088aa63-34ed-42f3-af9f-c844bc9c50b0)

In fact I did come up with this implementation by looking at the implementation of the `resolve_dependencies` rule in the graph. However getting the subset is only one step in the process, that results in getting the actual target generator, we still need the final generated target and it's why there is a call to `parametrizations.generated_for` right after.

Although exposing the subset will get a long way, because IIRC there is a dependency relationship between a target generator and its generated targets, so I guess I could resolve the dependencies of the generator and, in case I find none or more than one, then error there.

In a way, the field `artifact` in `scalac_plugin` is like a `SingleDependencyField` for which we expect to find only one target after expanding parameters and target generation. What makes it even more awkward is the fact that the _consumer target_, the one that carries the parameters over, is not the `scalac_plugin` itself, but the `scala_sources` target that lists the plugin names in the `scalar_plugins` field (a field that gives me `SpecialCasedDependencies` vibes, but that's a different topic).

So, in a way, everything here smells of this being _a special case_:
1. A target (`scalac_plugin`) that adds metadata to the build and it's meant to _decorate_ another target (`jvm_artifact`/`scala_artifact`).
1. A 1:1 target generator (`scala_artifact`->`jvm_artifact`)
1. Parametrized fields
1. A _compile-only dependency_ between a `scala_source(s)` target and the `jvm_artifact` target generated, and the dependency happens via an intermediary target (the `scalac_plugin`)
1. Also, Scalac plugins can be globally defined in the `scalac` subsystem using a `dict` where the key is the resolve name.

In general, this is hard to generalise as I can only the Kotlin backend implements compiler plugins in a similar fashion and it's unlikely this scenario shows up in there too.

Ok, I had a look at the 2 CI failures. It looks like actually, the tests are badly constructed!

@kba should weigh in, but my analysis is the following:
1. `TestDummyProcessor.test_copies_ok` create a temporary WS, calls `run_processor` on it, then checks its outputs. One of these tests uses `page_from_file` on the OcrdFile outputs. But naturally, `page_from_file` assumes that we are still in the WS directory – which formally, we are not. But until now, `run_processor` (via `Processor.__init__`) **did chdir to the WS and stayed there**. But if we move the getcwd to before `get_processor`, then the new `finally` clause will undo that. So really, we should rewrite the test to chdir to `wsdir` from the beginning
2. `TestDummyProcessor.test_copy_file_false` ditto.

```python
>>> import pickle
>>> def foo(): pass

>>> dumped = pickle.dumps(bar)

{'foo': <function foo at 0x7fc3a19c6598>}

b'\x80\x03}q\x00X\x03\x00\x00\x00fooq\x01c__main__\nfoo\nq\x02s.'
```

@avnishn I think we could introduce another mode of operation for the async request manager for such blocking operations like `sync_filters_if_needed` that checks for the health of actors with a dummy function while calling.

`sync_filters_if_needed` is only called once or twice per training iteration. Such calls are very distinct from our experience sampling calls in that they are very few and small, but all of them should go through if RolloutWorkers are alive, as opposed to our sampling calls which are many and heavy, and we don't care if 1-2 don't make it in time.

So another mode we could introduce here would be calling a function like `_sync_filters_if_needed` and pinging with `actor.apply.remote(lambda_fn)` in parallel for example every minute. If the ping times out, we know at least that the targeted worker is dead and we can disregard the request and move on. 

I know this solution does not sound well engineered, but I think it could be a KISS way of dealing with this because I suppose that most of the time we risk waiting 60 minutes, the worker will simply be dead. It would also add very little logic and would be able to send meaningful warnings.

I don't think the above situation is an issue.  
- After any backoff delay the radio should perform CA check on the same channel. 
- It is also the same behavior with any radio platform (RCP or not).
- OT core will always issue a `otPlatRadioReceive()` after any frame tx (success or failed) from `UpdateIdleMode()`. 

The `RadioSpinel`/RCP corner case I can think of (which should still work fine) is if RCP hangs/resets in middle of a frame tx and we have the "RCP reset recovery" feature enabled (i.e. `OPENTHREAD_SPINEL_CONFIG_RCP_RESTORATION_MAX_COUNT > 0`). 
- In this case the "RCP recovery logic" will set the channel to the frame tx channel.
- But it should be ok, since from OT stack perspective we are in middle of tx (on that channel) and expect a `txdone` callback from which we will call `UpdateIdleMode()` and set the channel to whatever PAN channel is (or basically what OT stack wants it to be).

What I'm saying is signal handling is an independent problem from watching child process. The old watcher uses signal handling and will crash when mixed with xdist. The new watcher doesn't use signal handling and therefore is not problematic.

For reference, here's cpython's implementation for `ThreadedChildWatcher` https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/3.8/Lib/asyncio/unix_events.py#L1296. It starts a new thread and calls `waitpid`.

Compare that with `SafeChildWatcher` which inherits from `BaseChildWatcher` https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/3.8/Lib/asyncio/unix_events.py#L927. It tries to register signal handler and hence will raise an exception in non-main thread.

Yeah, as explained above, handling ambiguity will be really important (fine in followup). I suspect there are 3 sources of ambiguity:

- \>1 first party target w/ same import path
- \>1 third party target w/ same import path (is this possible in Go?)
- first party and third party refer to same import path

The Python code in `dependency_inference/` is a good example of how to handle this. Basically, the import path mappings will keep track of which import paths are ambiguous. They don't eagerly warn, only keep track. Then, your rule to map a particular Go import path to its target will keep track of ambiguous vs. unambiguous owners:

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/650a9b3cd26bc48751e2c9e570699b6779dbfc0b/src/python/pants/backend/python/dependency_inference/module_mapper.py#L318-L384

Finally, you'd use this code to try to disambiguate and then warn:

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/650a9b3cd26bc48751e2c9e570699b6779dbfc0b/src/python/pants/backend/python/dependency_inference/rules.py#L149-L165 

There's some added complexity in that we allow for Python to sometimes have 2 owners of the same module when using type stubs. You don't need that complexity, sounds like there should either be 1 owner or it's ambiguous.
